content: I am a PhD student at the Vancouver School of Economics at the University of British Columbia. My fields of interests are labor economics and applied econometrics, with a particular focus on informality in labor markets, and difference-in-differences designs. I received a Master in Economics from UBC and a Bachelor in Economics from Universidad de Piura.
summary: I am a PhD student at the Vancouver School of Economics at the University of British Columbia. My fields of interests are labor economics and applied econometrics, with a particular focus on informality in labor markets, and difference-in-differences designs.
title: Paul Corcuera
Distribution of Treatment Effects in Difference-in-differences Designs with Multiple Time Periods

summary: I investigate the earnings, employment, and reallocation effects of an increase in the Peruvian national minimum wage that occurred in 2016. Additionally, I examine the effects that this policy had on other household outcomes such as labor supply of the spouse, schooling decisions of the teenagers living at the household, and spending responses. Using an empirical strategy based on a difference-in-differences design, I decompose the effects along the earnings distribution. I find that low-wage workers who were earning close to the old minimum wage experienced more than a  18% increase in earnings, and around 25.4% increase relative to workers in higher parts of the earnings distribution. Thus the minimum wage hike was successful in decreasing earnings inequality. At the same time, I find no evidence of negative employment effects nor reallocation of workers from the formal sector towards the informal sector. On households where the head was a low-wage worker, this policy had null effects on labor supply for either the spouse or teenagers living in the household. Moreover, following the minimum wage hike I find that households allocate a higher share of household income towards rent and household services.
title: The Consequences of Minimum Wage Hikes in Developing Countries:  Evidence from Peru
